name: user-sovereignty
description: The principle that individual users retain ultimate authority over their software environment — execution

name: collective-sovereignty
description: A community's right to govern shared software commons and infrastructure; potentially in tension with individual user sovereignty.

name: formal-freedom
description: Legal and technical permission to modify/inspect/fork/redistribute — necessary but not sufficient for practical freedom; elevated to document-wide cross-cutting framework in cycle 002.

name: practical-freedom
description: The actual exercisable capacity to modify/inspect/fork given real-world constraints of skills
column9: and time; the operative standard under this proposal.

name: tivoization
description: Hardware using free software but locking users out of running modified versions via firmware verification; listed as anti-pattern.

name: cloudization
description: [speculative] Systems where hardware is local but meaningful computation runs remotely — cloud-era mutation of tivoization.

name: network-lock-in
description: Emergent dependency created by network effects even when code is technically free; makes alternatives impractical.

name: freedom-commons
description: Model treating freedom-preserving software as public infrastructure requiring commons governance rather than individual or vendor control.

name: data-sovereignty
description: User rights over their own generated data — distinct from code freedom and may require different protections; addressed within the end-user domain of the layered sovereignty model.

name: composability-paradox
description: [speculative] Composable tools that create emergent opacity and lock-in at the system level even when components are individually free.

name: training-data-extraction
description: Free-to-use systems that harvest user behavior to train proprietary models; extracting cognitive labor without reciprocation.

name: complexity-moat
description: Open-source code made practically unmodifiable through deliberate or emergent architectural complexity and missing documentation; generalized to a document-wide anti-pattern in cycle 002.

name: positive-freedom
description: Freedom to accomplish — active capacity enabled by tooling/documentation/community support; contrasts with negative freedom from restriction; operationalized as positive-architectural-obligation in cycle 002.

name: negative-freedom
description: Freedom from restriction or constraint; the primary register of the GNU Free Software Definition.

name: fork-fragmentation
description: Risk that individual exercise of the right to fork fragments a community commons that depends on network effects.

name: inspectability-by-default
description: Principle that all meaningful system behavior should be understandable and observable — readable source
column8: durable logs; extended with AI-inspectability criteria in cycle 002.

name: right-to-fork
description: Principle that communities must be able to continue a system independently of its original creator; treated as resilience infrastructure.

name: shared-improvement-loop
description: Principle that improvements compound socially through contribution
column10: and publication of derived work; dependency on practical modifiability made explicit in cycle 002.

name: modifiability-first-class
description: Treating user ability to adapt systems as a core architectural requirement — modular components
column8: portable formats; extended with testable criterion and positive obligations in cycle 002.

name: forkability-as-resilience
description: Treating the ability to fork as infrastructure for community resilience; architectural implication added: core value should be protocol-level not concentration-level to survive fragmentation.

name: layered-sovereignty
description: A governance model that resolves multi-party sovereignty by assigning domain-specific rights to end users
column8: and organizations with a priority rule that no layer may eliminate a lower layer's rights within that lower layer's domain.

name: freedom-activation-energy
description: The threshold of skill
column9: and knowledge required before a formally-permitted freedom becomes practically exercisable; reducing activation energy is a positive design obligation.

name: positive-architectural-obligation
description: The obligation on system builders to actively enable the exercise of freedoms through architecture — progressive disclosure
column9: test infrastructure — beyond merely refraining from restricting them.

name: AI-inspectability
description: Conditions under which AI-powered systems satisfy inspectability-by-default: auditable training lineage
column9: surfaced uncertainty; distinguishes from post-hoc saliency explanations. Cycle 003: criteria flagged as structurally dependent on vendor self-attestation with no external verification mechanism.

name: governance-capture
description: The failure mode in which multi-stakeholder governance bodies are dominated by the well-resourced actors they are meant to check — achieved through funding of full-time participants and agenda-setting rather than formal veto.

name: compliance-sovereignty-conflict
description: The structural tension between regulatory compliance obligations (GDPR/HIPAA/SOC2) and user sovereignty as defined in this proposal; in regulated industries binding law requires restricting exactly the freedoms the layered sovereignty priority rule protects.

name: unfunded-obligation
description: The failure mode in which positive architectural obligations imposed on builders without a corresponding funding model select for well-resourced actors and disadvantage under-resourced projects — potentially concentrating the power the framework was designed to check.

name: freedom-decay
description: The gradual erosion of practical freedom through processes requiring no deliberate decision — stale documentation
column10: and growing codebase complexity. Cycle 005: promoted from emergent; concrete decay indicators added (documentation staleness
column14: codebase comprehension ceiling); periodic assessment mechanism defined with inputs

name: infrastructure-dependency-stack
description: The layered infrastructure below the application layer (hardware
column9: infrastructure services) whose freedom properties are inherited by applications built on top of them. Cycle 005: promoted from emergent/speculative; concrete design implication added — systems must document their infrastructure dependency footprint with governance status of each dependency; preference for commons-governed dependencies elevated to requirement; identified as primary accelerant of freedom-decay.

name: voluntary-delegation
description: The mechanism by which users actively choose to surrender freedoms in exchange for convenience
column8: or social participation — distinct from coercive lock-in. Cycle 005: promoted from emergent; committed to structural coercion framing (interpretation 3) as operative assumption; design implication specified — the system's obligation is to ensure genuine alternatives exist (ecosystem model)
column9: not to prevent freedom-reducing choices (prohibition model); empirical distinguishing test added.

name: reversibility
description: The property of systems that allows users to undo state changes
column8: and restore prior capability without data loss. Cycle 005: promoted from emergent/speculative; defined precisely as an individual-temporal mechanism distinct from forkability (collective continuity)
column10: and exit rights (permission mechanism); integrated as sub-principle of User Sovereignty; four testable criteria added (state undo

name: contributor-obligations
description: The reciprocal duties that users who benefit from a freedom-preserving commons have toward sustaining it. Cycle 005: promoted from emergent/speculative; distinguished hard obligations (attribution
column7: non-weaponization — enforceable through license or governance policy) from soft norms (upkeep contribution — reinforced through community culture); rationale for distinction provided: upkeep as hard obligation produces unfunded-obligation anti-pattern; attribution and non-weaponization are binary and low-cost
column8: their absence directly degrades the commons.
